title: CMMC SI.L2-3.14.6 — Real-time attack monitoring on CUI systems

compliance.assurance_rationale: SPL correlates EDR and IDS detections on CUI systems for real-time attack monitoring — directly satisfying SI.L2-3.14.6.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CMMC SI.L2-3.14.6 (Monitor for attacks) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.20.9: CMMC Level 2 practice evidence — CUI control area 9.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.20.9' running on WinEventLog:Security, CrowdStrike FDR, Defender for Office 365, NetFlow, encryption posture exports, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: CrowdStrike detections, Microsoft Defender alerts, Carbon Black alerts, Suricata IDS, Snort IDS, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup
app: Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).
spl:
(index=endpoint sourcetype IN ("crowdstrike:detections", "defender:alerts", "carbon_black:alerts") earliest=-24h)
OR (index=ids sourcetype IN ("suricata", "snort") earliest=-24h)
| lookup cui_hosts.csv host OUTPUT cui_flag
| where cui_flag="true"
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, alert_severity, priority)
| eval attack_type=coalesce(tactic, category, classification)
| stats count AS detections dc(host) AS affected_hosts values(attack_type) AS attack_types latest(_time) AS last_seen BY severity
| sort case(severity="critical",0, severity="high",1, severity="medium",2, true(),3)
| table severity, detections, affected_hosts, attack_types, last_seen
description: Correlates real-time attack indicators from EDR (CrowdStrike, Defender, Carbon Black) and IDS (Suricata, Snort) on CUI-classified systems. CMMC SI.L2-3.14.6 requires monitoring organisational systems to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks.
value: Provides a unified attack monitoring view across CUI systems combining endpoint detection and network intrusion data — demonstrating continuous, multi-layer attack monitoring capability.
implementation: (1) Verify data sources: CrowdStrike detections, Microsoft Defender alerts, Carbon Black alerts, Suricata IDS, Snort IDS, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup; (2) Maintain CUI-scoped lookups; (3) Schedule search at compliance-aligned interval; (4) Route findings to ticketing queue; (5) Retain exports per records management policy.
visualization: Table of detections by severity. Timeline of detection events. Single value for total critical/high severity detections in last 24 hours.
cimSpl: | tstats count from datamodel=Intrusion_Detection BY IDS_Attacks.severity, IDS_Attacks.dest | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Vulnerability scanners and penetration testing tools generate detection events — correlate with approved scan schedules.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CrowdStrike detections, Microsoft Defender alerts, Carbon Black alerts, Suricata IDS, Snort IDS, `cui_hosts.csv` lookup.
• Install and configure: Splunk Add-on for CrowdStrike FDR (5082), Splunk Enterprise Security (263).

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify that all required data sources are ingesting into Splunk. Maintain the CUI-scoped lookups (cui_hosts.csv, cui_network_segments.csv, etc.) with current asset inventory.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL (save as report or alert):

```spl
(index=endpoint sourcetype IN ("crowdstrike:detections", "defender:alerts", "carbon_black:alerts") earliest=-24h)
OR (index=ids sourcetype IN ("suricata", "snort") earliest=-24h)
| lookup cui_hosts.csv host OUTPUT cui_flag
| where cui_flag="true"
| eval severity=coalesce(severity, alert_severity, priority)
| eval attack_type=coalesce(tactic, category, classification)
| stats count AS detections dc(host) AS affected_hosts values(attack_type) AS attack_types latest(_time) AS last_seen BY severity
| sort case(severity="critical",0, severity="high",1, severity="medium",2, true(),3)
| table severity, detections, affected_hosts, attack_types, last_seen
```

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm expected results against known CUI systems. Verify lookup tables are populated and field extractions are correct.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Schedule the search at an appropriate interval. Route findings to the compliance ticketing queue. Table of detections by severity. Timeline of detection events. Single value for total critical/high severity detections in last 24 hours.
grandmaExplanation: We watch for actual attacks happening on computers that handle sensitive government data, using multiple security tools working together. If anything suspicious happens, we see it in real time.
